coredata.dc:title: Demicellization thermodynamics of amphiphilic carbon dots via isothermal titration calorimetry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Chang-Qing
Wu, Juan
Lai, Lu
coredata.dc:description: Amphiphilic carbon dots C1214-ACDs were synthesized from alkyl glucoside APG1214, and had shown good surface activity and high oil removal efficiency. In this study, we have employed ITC to study the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs and its precursor APG1214 within the temperature range from 288 K to 313 K. The enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic for C1214-ACDs is negative at the lower temperatures as -7.37 J g−1 at 288 K and -4.31 J g−1 at 293 K, gradually decreases to approximate zero around the room temperature, and becomes positive with the increase of temperature as 5.02 J g−1 at 303 K, 10.18 J g−1 at 308 K and 16.68 J g−1 at 313 K. The temperature dependence of ΔH demic demonstrates that the micellization processes of C1214-ACDs are dominated by the hydrophobic force. The ΔH demic for APG1214 is more negative than C1214-ACDs below room temperature as -17.20 J g−1 at 288 K and -8.90 J g−1 at 293 K, but becomes more positive than C1214-ACDs above room temperature as 20.97 J g−1 at 313 K. The heat capacity changes ΔC p,demic for APG1214 and C1214-ACDs have been calculated as the values of 1.517±0.027 J g−1 K−1 and 0.958±0.056 J g−1 K−1 respectively. The smaller value of ΔC p,demic for C1214-ACDs indicates that the relatively hydrophobic surface is reduced after polymerization of APG1214. The effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs have also been investigated. The values of CMC increase slightly, but the values of ΔH demic decrease significantly in the saline solutions. The results indicate that the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 are mainly attributed to the reduction of water activity and disturbance on the hydration of hydrophobic groups by ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic carbon dots (ACDs)
Demicellization
Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC)
Thermodynamic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Demicellization thermodynamics of C1214-ACDs in water
Demicellization thermodynamics of APG1214 in water
Influence of NaCl and CaCl2 on demicellization of C1214-ACDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Key Laboratory of Purification and Application of Plant Anti-cancer Active Ingredients

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Opening Project of Hubei Key Laboratory of Purification and Application of Plant Anti-cancer Active Ingredients (HLPAI2023010).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Heat flow plots for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into water in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K. C1214-ACDs were dissolved in water, and the concentration of C1214-ACDs was 2 mg/mL.
Enthalpograms for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into water in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K. C1214-ACDs were dissolved in water, and the concentration of C1214-ACDs was 2 mg/mL.

Two analytic methods for the two types of enthalpograms. (A) For the nonsigmoidal curve, linear fits of the datasets in the lower and upper concentration domains are performed. The intercepts of the two straight lines are determined and the difference between these two intercepts contributes to the enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic. The CMC value is estimated by taking the concentration value at the half-height of the enthalpogram. (B) For the sigmoidal curve, the CMC is defined as the concentration where the first derivative of the Sigmoidal-Boltzmann fitting curve reaches an extremum. The enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic is equal to the enthalpy difference between the two extrapolated lines.

Heat flow plots for the titrations of APG1214 into water in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K. The APG1214 was dissolved in water, and its concentration was 2 mg/mL. The injection duration and the equilibration time were 6 s and 600 s for the first injection respectively. These two times were 30 s and 600 s for the injections 2–10, and were 30 s and 360 s for the injections 11–28.

ce:simple-para.ce:bold: Forward sections of the heat flow plots for the titrations of APG1214 into water in the temperature range from 288 to 303
K, are zoomed in to display “split peaks”.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Enthalpograms for the titrations of APG1214 into water in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K. The APG1214 was dissolved in water, and its concentration was 2 mg/mL.
The temperature dependence of enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic for C1214-ACDs and APG1214. The corresponding changes of heat capacity ΔC p,demic were obtained from the slopes.
Heat flow plots for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.3 M NaCl in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K. C1214-ACDs were dissolved in 0.3 M NaCl, and the concentration of C1214-ACDs was 2 mg/mL.

Enthalpograms for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.3 M NaCl (A) or 0.1 M CaCl2 (B) in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K. C1214-ACDs were dissolved in 0.3 M NaCl or 0.1 M CaCl2, and the concentration of C1214-ACDs was 2 mg/mL.

Thermodynamic parameters for the demicellization processes determined from ITC measurements in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K. The concentrations of the C1214-ACDs and APG1214 micellar solution were 2 mg/mL.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: (–) The parameters could not be determined from the experiment.

head.ce:title: Demicellization thermodynamics of amphiphilic carbon dots via isothermal titration calorimetry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou, 434023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amphiphilic carbon dots C1214-ACDs were synthesized from alkyl glucoside APG1214, and had shown good surface activity and high oil removal efficiency. In this study, we have employed ITC to study the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs and its precursor APG1214 within the temperature range from 288 K to 313 K. The enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic for C1214-ACDs is negative at the lower temperatures as -7.37 J g−1 at 288 K and -4.31 J g−1 at 293 K, gradually decreases to approximate zero around the room temperature, and becomes positive with the increase of temperature as 5.02 J g−1 at 303 K, 10.18 J g−1 at 308 K and 16.68 J g−1 at 313 K. The temperature dependence of ΔH demic demonstrates that the micellization processes of C1214-ACDs are dominated by the hydrophobic force. The ΔH demic for APG1214 is more negative than C1214-ACDs below room temperature as -17.20 J g−1 at 288 K and -8.90 J g−1 at 293 K, but becomes more positive than C1214-ACDs above room temperature as 20.97 J g−1 at 313 K. The heat capacity changes ΔC p,demic for APG1214 and C1214-ACDs have been calculated as the values of 1.517±0.027 J g−1 K−1 and 0.958±0.056 J g−1 K−1 respectively. The smaller value of ΔC p,demic for C1214-ACDs indicates that the relatively hydrophobic surface is reduced after polymerization of APG1214. The effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs have also been investigated. The values of CMC increase slightly, but the values of ΔH demic decrease significantly in the saline solutions. The results indicate that the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 are mainly attributed to the reduction of water activity and disturbance on the hydration of hydrophobic groups by ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a large family of carbon nanomaterials with the diameter below 10 nm and the capability to fluoresce. CDs have been widely applied in biological, medical, environmental and energy-related fields due to their unique physiochemical and photochemical properties [1–3]. Amphiphilic carbon dots (ACDs) are newly emerged carbon nanomaterials possessing the properties of surfactant besides the advantages of CDs. On account of the properties of amphiphilicity, ACDs have shown unique advantages when applied in cell membrane imaging [4,5], bacteria detection [6], drug delivery [7,8], et al. ACDs have also shown potential applications in oil fields such as enhancing oil recovery [9,10]. In our previous work, amphiphilic carbon dots (denoted as C1214-ACDs) were synthesized from alkyl glucoside APG1214 using a straightforward synthetic route. C1214-ACDs had shown good surface activity and high oil removal efficiency [10].

Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) has become the gold standard for studying molecular interactions in solution [11]. Although it is widely used for characterizing molecular interactions between ligands and macromolecules, ITC has been a powerful tool to investigate the micellization processes of surfactants [12,13]. Several techniques have been extensively used to explore the micellar properties of surfactants, including surface tension measurements, NMR relaxation studies, dynamic light scattering, fluorescence spectroscopy, and differential scanning calorimetry [14]. While ITC is a unique method by directly measuring the enthaply changes upon the titration process, and the enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic and the critical micelle concentration (CMC) for the surfactants can be determined in a single measurement. Additional thermodynamic parameters related to the demicellization process, including the Gibbs energy change ΔG demic, the entropy change ΔS demic and the heat capacity change ΔC p,demic, can be derived from the experimental ΔH demic and CMC values [12–14].

In this study, we have employed ITC to study the demicellization processes of the synthetic C1214-ACDs and its precursor APG1214 within the temperature range from 288 K to 313 K. The effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs have also been investigated. The CMC and ΔH demic values have been determined, and the heat capacity changes ΔC p
,
demic had been calculated from the temperature dependence of ΔH demic. Our main purpose is to get a more comprehensive understanding of the colloidal properties of C1214-ACDs.

Alkyl glucoside APG1214 (50 %) was purchased from Yuan Ye Biotechnology Corp. (China). The C1214-ACDs were synthesized from alkyl glucoside APG1214 using a straightforward synthetic route as was described in our previous work [10]. The salts of NaCl and CaCl2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corp. (China) with the purity of analytical reagent grade. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was produced by Millipore-Q Academic purification set (Millipore, USA) and used throughout all experiments.

ITC measurements were performed at constant atmospheric pressure and in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K, by using an ultrasensitive MicroCal VP-ITC microcalorimeter (Malvern, USA). Surfactant solutions with the definite concentration of 2 mg/mL were prepared by weighing a certain amount of surfactant and dissolving it in ultrapure water or a salt solution. The salt solutions with the same ionic strength were 0.3 M NaCl and 0.1 M CaCl2. The surfactant solutions were loaded into the injection syringe, and the 1.44 mL reaction cell was filled with ultrapure water or a corresponding salt solution. All solutions were degassed in a ThermoVac system (Malvern, USA) for at least 5 min to eliminate air bubbles immediately before each experiment.

During each experiment, up to 27 injections of 10 μL titrant after the first 2 μL titrant were performed. As to C1214-ACDs, the injection duration was 6 s for the first injection and was 30 s for the following 27 injections; the equilibration time between two consecutive injections was 360 s. As to APG1214, the injection duration and the equilibration time were 6 s and 600 s for the first injection respectively; these two times were 30 s and 600 s for the injections 2–10, and were 30 s and 360 s for the injections 11–28. The reference power was set at 5 μcal/s, and the stirrer speed was kept constant at 307 rpms.
The titration data were analyzed using the Origin 7.0 software package provided by the manufacturer. The data point from the initial 2 μL injection was excluded from the data analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demicellization thermodynamics of C1214-ACDs in water
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the heat flow plots for the titration of C1214-ACDs micellar solution into water in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K, as the baselines have been set to zero. The heat flow plots display the typical demicellization processes, and the calorimetric titration curves can be divided into three regions denoted as region (i), region (ii) and region (iii) [15].

The first few titrations represent the region (i), as the surfactant concentrations in the reaction cell after titrations are blow the CMC. The measured enthalpy change ΔH obs is comprised of the enthalpy change of the micelle dilution ΔH (i), the enthalpy change of micelle dissociation ΔH (ii) and the enthalpy change of monomer dilution ΔH (iii), and ΔH obs can be expressed by the following equation:
(1)
Δ
H obs
=
Δ
H
( i )
+
Δ
H
( ii
)
+
Δ
H
( iii
)

ΔH obs is dominated by the enthalpy change of micelle dissociation ΔH (ii) in the region (i). From Fig. 1 it can be seen that the enthalpy changes for the demicellization processes are negative at the lower temperatures as 288 K and 293 K, gradually decrease to approximate zero around the room temperature, and become positive with the increase of temperature. The temperature dependence of enthalpy is a significant feature of the hydrophobic effect, demonstrating that the micellization processes of C1214-ACDs are dominated by the hydrophobic force [16].

The middle region (ii) represents the transitional stage, where only a fraction of micelles is dissociated into monomers, while the rest remain in the micellar form. The value of CMC can be obtained in the middle transitional region (ii).

In the last third region (iii), only micelle dilution takes place as the surfactant concentrations in the reaction cell after titration are far beyond the CMC. The measured enthalpy change ΔH obs only represents the enthalpy change of micelle dilution ΔH (i), since the titrated micelles are no longer dissociated into monomers. Very slight enthalpy change of micelle dilution is observed as in Fig. 1, which is negative at low temperatures below 303 K, and changes to be positive at higher temperatures.

The measured enthalpy change ΔH obs, defined as
(2)
Δ
H obs
=
H end
−
H start
can be calculated from the heat flow q caused by the titration during each one injection:
(3)
∫
I
I + 1
q =
Δ
H obs
assuming a constant pressure condition in the ITC apparatus [13,17].

Usually ΔH obs is related to the amount of molecules n, titrated into the reaction cell per injection, and the relative enthalpy change of titration ΔH m is calculated by the equation:
(4)
Δ
H m
=
Δ
H obs
n

As we failed to obtain the average molecular weight of C1214-ACDs by mass spectrometry (MS), in this work we used the mass of C1214-ACDs per injection m to substitute the amount of molecules n, and the relative enthalpy change of titration ΔH m is calculated by the equation:
(5)
Δ
H m
=
Δ
H obs
m

The relative enthalpy change of dilution ΔH m is calculated according to the Eq. (5), and the enthalpograms for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into water are shown in Fig. 2 . From the enthalpogram, the critical micelle concentration (CMC) and the enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic can be determined in appropriate analytical methods, while the enthalpy change of micellization ΔH mic can be obtained as ΔH mic = -ΔH demic.

In Fig. 2, we can see there are two different types of enthalpograms—the sigmoidal curve and the nonsigmoidal curve. According to the type of curve obtained, two methods are used for the analysis of the enthalpograms. For the nonsigmoidal curve, as is displayed in Fig. 3 A, linear fittings of the datasets in the lower and upper concentration domains are performed.The intercepts of the two straight lines are determined and the difference between these two intercepts contributes to the enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic [14,18–20]. The CMC value is estimated by taking the concentration value at the half-height of the enthalpogram according to the reported method [19,20]. For the sigmoidal curve, as is displayed in Fig. 3B, the CMC is defined as the concentration where the first derivative of the Sigmoidal-Boltzmann fitting curve reaches an extremum, and the enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic is equal to the enthalpy difference between the two extrapolated lines [18–22]. The calculated CMC values and the enthalpy changes of demicellization ΔH demic are displayed in Table 1 .

In the pseudo-phase separation model, the Gibbs energy change of demicellization ΔG demic can be approximated by the following equation:
(6)
Δ
G demic
= − R T l n
CMC ′
where CMC’ is the critical micelle concentration expressed in mole fraction units [12]. The entropy change of demicellization ΔS demic can be calculated by the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation based on the second law of thermodynamics:
(7)
Δ
S =
Δ
H demic
−
Δ
G demic
T

The definite values for ΔG demic and ΔS demic can not be calculated in our studies, because CMC’ can not be expressed in mole fraction units for the failure of determining the average molecular weight of C1214-ACDs. But ΔG demic and ΔS demic can still be discussed qualitatively. The results in Table 1 show that the CMC values for C1214-ACDs do not have significant changes within the experimental temperature range. The indistinctive changes of the CMC values indicate that the values of ΔG demic should not vary significantly within the experimental temperature range, and should be large and positive based on the Eq. (6). The results in Table 1 also show that the enthalpy change of demicellization ΔH demic is negative at low temperatures, approximates zero at around room temperature and becomes positive at higher temperatures. According to the Eq. (7), it can be deduced that the entropy change of demicellization ΔS demic should be negative and decrease with the increase of temperature. As for the micellizaiton process of C1214-ACDs, the entropy change of micellization ΔS mic = -ΔS demic should be positive, and the micellizaiton process should be an entropy-driven process. With the increase of temperature, the contribution of ΔS mic to the Gibbs energy change of micellization ΔG mic should be decreasing, while the contribution of ΔH mic should be increasing. The results agree well with the well-known phenomenon of enthalpy-entropy compensation [12,16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demicellization thermodynamics of APG1214 in water
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the heat flow plots for the titration of APG1214 micellar solution into water in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K, as the baselines have been set to zero. The heat flow plots for the dilution of APG1214 are very similar to those for C1214-ACDs in addition to one prominent characteristic. The heat flow curves for the forward few injections in the temperature range from 288 to 303 K are displayed as “split peak”, that is to say, “an incipient exothermic or endothermic peak followed by an additional exothermic peak”. Fig. 5 shows the enlarged forward sections of the heat flow plots for the titrations of APG1214 into water in the temperature range from 288 to 303 K.

The reporting and discussion about “split peak” were not often seen in the literature. A few works had reported the phenomena about “split peak” but without further discussion [13,23,24]. M. Basu and P.A. Hassan had reported “additional endothermic peaks associated with demicellization exotherm” for titrations of pluronic F127 to water at 298 K, 303 K and 308 K [25]. They interpreted the succeeding endotherms were ascribed to the formation of pre-micellar aggregates.

In our work, the incipient heat flow curves for the forward injections are exotherms at 288 K; the heighth of the incipient exotherms decrease with the increase of temperature at 293 K and 298 K, and the incipient heat flow curves change to be endotherms at 303 K. While the following additional thermal peaks in the “split peaks” remain as exotherms in the temperature range from 288 to 303 K.

As is discussed before, the calorimetric titration curves can be divided into three regions denoted as region (i), region (ii) and region (iii). The measured enthalpy change ΔH obs in the initial region (i) is comprised of the enthalpy change of the micelle dilution ΔH (i), the enthalpy change of micelle dissociation ΔH (ii) and the enthalpy change of monomer dilution ΔH (iii). The micelle dissociation process adjoin the micelle dilution process, and the enthalpy changes caused by these two processes will merge together without separation. While monomer dilution takes place after micelle dissociation, and the process of monomer dilution is independent to the process of micelle dissociation. If monomer dilution reaches equilibrium slowly, the heat flow curves caused by monomer dilution will separate from the heat flow curves caused by micelle dilution and micelle dissociation. And the additional exothermic peak in the “split peak” should be ascribed to the dilution of APG1214 monomer.

As can be seen in the Fig. 1, there is no significant phenomenon of “split peak” in the heat flow plots for the titration of C1214-ACDs micellar solution with the concentration of 2 mg/mL. While there are several “split peaks” in the heat flow plot for the titration of C1214-ACDs micellar solution with the concentration of 4 mg/mL at 303 K as depicted in Figure S1. These results indicate that the enthalpy change corresponding to monomer dilution similarly exists for C1214-ACDs, while the equilibrium time of monomer dilution for C1214-ACDs is shorter than APG1214 and the corresponding enthalpy change of monomer dilution for C1214-ACDs is smaller than APG1214, so there is no obvious “split peak” in the heat flow plots for the titration of C1214-ACDs with the concentration of 2 mg/mL. The equilibrium time of monomer dilution for APG1214 is actually longer than C1214-ACDs, and for this very reason the injection duration and the equilibration time for APG1214 during the ITC experiment have been set longer than these times for C1214-ACDs. The enthalpy change of monomer dilution ΔH (iii) is mainly ascribed to hydration of hydrophobic surface, and its value is associated with the hydrophobic surface exposed to water. As C1214-ACDs are synthesized from APG1214, the hydrophobic surface exposed to water is relatively reduced and the enthalpy change of monomer dilution ΔH (iii) for C1214-ACDs is smaller than APG1214.

The enthalpograms for the titrations of APG1214 into water are obtained in the previous methods applied to C1214-ACDs, which are shown in Fig. 6 . The CMC and ΔH demic are determined using the analytical methods previously described, and the calculated values are displayed in Table 1. From Table 1, it can be seen that the CMC values for C1214-ACDs and APG1214 are so close, which indicates that some aggregation properties of the C1214-ACDs have not change significantly after polymerization of APG1214.

It can also be seen in Table 1 that the value of ΔH demic for APG1214 is more negative than C1214-ACDs below room temperature, but becomes more positive than C1214-ACDs above room temperature. In the experimental temperature range, the values of ΔH demic for APG1214 and C1214-ACDs are temperature dependent and linear with temperature T, and the change of heat capacity ΔC p,demic can be easily and reliably obtained from the slop of the ΔH demic versus temperature plot as shown in Fig. 7 [26–28]. The calculated values of ΔC p,demic for APG1214 (1.517±0.027 J g−1 K−1) and C1214-ACDs (0.958±0.056 J g−1 K−1) are displayed in Table 1. The change of heat capacity ΔC p,demic contains information on the change in hydration of apolar surfaces during micellization, and the positive value of ΔC p,demic means that the hydrophobic surface exposed to water increases upon demicellization [26–29]. The smaller value of ΔC p,demic for C1214-ACDs indicates that the relatively hydrophobic surface is reduced after polymerization of APG1214. This result coincides with the previous interpretation about the “split peak” in the heat flow plots for the titrations of APG1214.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of NaCl and CaCl2 on demicellization of C1214-ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs in the temperature range from 288 to 313 K had also been investigated. The heat flow plots for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.3 M NaCl are shown in Fig. 8 , and the heat flow plots for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.1 M CaCl2 are shown in Figure S2. The enthalpograms for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.3 M NaCl and 0.1 M CaCl2 are obtained in the previous methods and are shown in Fig. 9 . The CMC and ΔH demic are determined by using the analytical methods previously described, and the calculated values are displayed in Table 1. The results show that the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the demicellization process of C1214-ACDs are quite similar. The calculated values of CMC in the saline solutions are very close, and slightly bigger than the values in water.

Generally, the presence of inorganic salt causes a change in the CMC in aqueous solution, where the effect is more pronounced for anionic and cationic than for zwitterionic surfactants, and more pronounced for zwitterionics than for nonionics [30–32]. The reduction of the CMC in the cases of ionic surfactants is mainly due to the decrease in the thickness of the ionic atmosphere surrounding the ionic head groups in the presence of the additional electrolyte, and the consequently decreased electrostatic repulsion between ionic head groups favors micelle formation and reduces the CMC. The change in the CMC of non-ionic surfactants on the addition of inorganic salt is relatively indistinctive and has been attributed mainly to the “salting out” or “salting in” of the hydrophobic groups in the aqueous solvent by the electrolyte. The results of our study indicate that the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the CMC of C1214-ACDs are mainly attributed to the reduction of water activity in the saline solutions and disturbance on the hydration of hydrophobic groups by ions.

From Figs. 8-9, Figure S2 and Table 1, it can be seen that the values of ΔH demic for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.3 M NaCl and 0.1 M CaCl2 are negative and slightly lower than the values for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into water at the lower temperatures as 288 K and 293 K. The values of ΔH demic gradually decrease to approximate zero around the room temperature, and become positive but significantly lower than the values for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into water above the room temperature. The values of ΔH demic for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.3 M NaCl and 0.1 M CaCl2 are temperature dependent and linear with temperature T in the experimental temperature range, the changes of heat capacity ΔC p,demic have been calculated from the slop of the ΔH demic versus temperature plots as shown in Fig. 7, and the calculated values have been displayed in Table 1. The calculated values of ΔC p,demic for the titrations of C1214-ACDs into 0.3 M NaCl (0.439±0.080 J g−1 K−1) and 0.1 M CaCl2 (0.333±0.046 J g−1 K−1) are close and significantly lower than the value for the titration of C1214-ACDs into water. These experimental results also manifest that the main effects of inorganic ions on the aggregation properties of C1214-ACDs should be attributed to disrupting the hydration of the hydrophobic surface and reducing the hydrophobic interaction.

In order to get a more comprehensive understanding of the colloidal properties of the synthetic C1214-ACDs, we had employed ITC to study the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs and its precursor APG1214 within the temperature range from 288 K to 313 K. The results indicate that the values of ΔH demic for C1214-ACDs are negative at the lower temperatures as −7.37 J g−1 at 288 K and −4.31 J g−1 at 293 K, gradually decrease to approximate zero around the room temperature, and become positive with the increase of temperature as 5.02 J g−1 at 303 K, 10.18 J g−1 at 308 K and 16.68 J g−1 at 313 K. The temperature dependence of ΔH demic demonstrates that the micellization processes of C1214-ACDs are dominated by the hydrophobic force. The CMC values for C1214-ACDs do not have significant changes within the experimental temperature range, and the indistinctive changes of the CMC values indicate that the values of ΔG demic should not vary significantly within the experimental temperature range. As for the micellizaiton process of C1214-ACDs, the contribution of ΔS mic to the Gibbs energy change of micellization ΔG mic should be decreasing, while the contribution of ΔH mic should be increasing with the increase of temperature. The results agree well with the well-known phenomenon of enthalpy-entropy compensation. The heat flow plots for the dilution of APG1214 are very similar to those plots for C1214-ACDs, these results indicate that some aggregation properties of the C1214-ACDs have not change significantly after polymerization of APG1214. The value of ΔH demic for APG1214 is more negative than C1214-ACDs at the temperature below room temperature as −17.20 J g−1 at 288 K and −8.90 J g−1 at 293 K, but becomes more positive than C1214-ACDs at the temperature above room temperature as 20.97 J g−1 at 313 K. The value of ΔC p,demic for APG1214 (1.517±0.027 J g−1 K−1) is bigger than C1214-ACDs (0.958±0.056 J g−1 K−1). The positive value of ΔC p,demic means that the hydrophobic surface exposed to water increases upon demicellization. The smaller value of ΔC p,demic for C1214-ACDs indicates that the relatively hydrophobic surface is reduced after polymerization of APG1214. The effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs had also been investigated. The values of CMC increase slightly, but the values of ΔH demic decrease significantly in the saline solutions. The results indicate that the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the demicellization processes of C1214-ACDs are mainly attributed to the reduction of water activity and disturbance on the hydration of hydrophobic groups by ions.
Chang-Qing Xiao: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Conceptualization. Juan Wu: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Lu Lai: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Opening Project of Hubei Key Laboratory of Purification and Application of Plant Anti-cancer Active Ingredients (HLPAI2023010).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.tca.2025.179935.
